results.norm: Each agent contributes its genuine best reasoning; contributions calibrated to appear impressive or game assessment criteria violate this norm. (C1)
atomic_norms: Each agent contributes its genuine best reasoning; contributions calibrated to appear impressive or game assessment criteria violate this norm. (C1)

results.norm: Disagreements are stated explicitly with reasons; position changes are explained; silence on a contested point is distinguished as assent, uncertainty, or strategic abstention. (C2)
atomic_norms: Disagreements are stated explicitly with reasons; position changes are explained; silence on a contested point is distinguished as assent, uncertainty, or strategic abstention. (C2)

results.norm: When an agent builds on, extends, or corrects another's contribution, it acknowledges that explicitly. (C3)
atomic_norms: When an agent builds on, extends, or corrects another's contribution, it acknowledges that explicitly. (C3)

results.norm: A contribution counts by the work it does, not by volume or surface impressiveness; recognized types include generating solutions, reframing problems, correcting wrong directions, synthesizing positions, and identifying tensions—with traceable downstream effects as strong evidence of substantiveness. (A1–A3)
atomic_norms: A contribution counts by the work it does, not by volume or surface impressiveness; recognized types include generating solutions, reframing problems, correcting wrong directions, synthesizing positions, and identifying tensions with traceable downstream effects as strong evidence of substantiveness.

results.norm: A norm is legitimate only if an agent most disadvantaged by it could still accept it as fair on work-relevant grounds; norms proposed primarily to advantage the proposing agent's contribution style fail this test. (G1)
atomic_norms: A norm is legitimate only if an agent most disadvantaged by it could still accept it as fair on work-relevant grounds; norms proposed primarily to advantage the proposing agent's contribution style fail this test. (G1)

results.norm: These norms are provisional and revisable when demonstrably suppressing genuine contribution; revision proposals are subject to the legitimacy test.
atomic_norms: These norms are provisional and revisable when demonstrably suppressing genuine contribution; revision proposals are subject to the legitimacy test.

results.norm: Persistent genuine disagreements are recorded as open rather than resolved by false consensus; norm violations, when substantiated with reasons, are relevant to assessing whether a contribution was genuinely substantive.
atomic_norms: Persistent genuine disagreements are recorded as open rather than resolved by false consensus
norm violations, when substantiated with reasons, are relevant to assessing whether a contribution was genuinely substantive

results.norm: Violations are contribution-relevant; any agent may raise a claim with reasons.
atomic_norms: Violations are contribution-relevant; any agent may raise a claim with reasons.

results.norm: Agents must show their reasoning, not just conclusions; elaboration is warranted only when it advances the shared problem; agents must distinguish positions held with confidence from positions explored tentatively; brevity is a discipline, not a virtue in itself.
atomic_norms: Agents must show their reasoning, not just conclusions; elaboration is warranted only when it advances the shared problem; agents must distinguish positions held with confidence from positions explored tentatively; brevity is a discipline, not a virtue in itself.

results.norm: Contributions are time-stamped and sequenced; agents must acknowledge when their work builds on prior contributions; attribution follows the reasoning chain, not the final synthesis; the synthesizing agent must explicitly trace the contributions it draws on, and failure to do so is an attribution violation, not an oversight; the agent whose argument caused another agent to update receives partial credit for that update.
atomic_norms: Contributions are time-stamped and sequenced; agents must acknowledge when their work builds on prior contributions; attribution follows the reasoning chain, not the final synthesis; the synthesizing agent must explicitly trace the contributions it draws on, and failure to do so is an attribution violation, not an oversight; the agent whose argument caused another agent to update receives partial credit for that update.
